summary: Andrew Carnegie was a highly successful American industrialist who built a massive steel empire by focusing on cost control, modern innovations, and gifted management. Later in life, he dedicated himself to philanthropy, believing that wealth accumulated in the first phase of life should be given away to benefit society in the second.

principles.statement: An industrialist's life should be divided into two phases: accumulation of wealth and distribution of wealth.
principles.rationale: Wealth should ultimately be given away to benefit society, such as by funding public libraries, education, and peace missions.
principles.source_quote: In one phase, he accumulates as much wealth as he can. In the other, he gives it all away to benefit society.

principles.statement: Profits will take care of themselves if costs are carefully monitored.
principles.rationale: Operational efficiency and strict cost control naturally lead to profitability and position a company to survive economic downturns.
principles.source_quote: Profits would take care of themselves if costs were carefully monitored

principles.statement: Gifted managers are worth more than the physical assets they run.
principles.rationale: Human talent and effective management are the true drivers of a business's success, outweighing the value of the physical mills or equipment.
principles.source_quote: The presence of gifted managers was worth more than the actual mills they ran

frameworks.name: The Gospel of Wealth Life Phases
frameworks.when_to_apply: When planning the trajectory of a successful industrialist's life and fortune.
steps: Accumulate as much wealth as you can during the first phase of your life.
Give all of the accumulated wealth away to benefit society in the second phase of your life.
frameworks.source_quote: In one phase, he accumulates as much wealth as he can. In the other, he gives it all away to benefit society.

mental_models.name: Counter-Cyclical Expansion
mental_models.description: Maintaining superior operational efficiency to ensure you are in the strongest financial position to acquire competitors and expand during economic downturns.
mental_models.example: Because his mills ran more efficiently than the competition, Carnegie was in the best position to snap up competing mills and companies when the economy hit a six-year slump in 1873.

heuristics: Monitor costs carefully and profits will take care of themselves.
Value gifted managers over the physical mills they run.
